title: Appendix: Matrix Calculus
blurb: The math behind backpropagation: gradients vs Jacobians, why a scalar loss keeps every gradient the same shape as its variable, and the affine-layer derivatives $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W}=X^\top\text{dout}$, $\frac{\partial L}{\partial X}=\text{dout}\,W^\top$, $\frac{\partial L}{\partial b}=\sum_n\text{dout}$.

knowledge_points.title: Gradients, Jacobians & the Scalar-Loss Principle
knowledge_points.recap: For $f:\mathbb{R}^{n\times m}\to\mathbb{R}$ (scalar output), $\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}$ is a **gradient** with the **same shape** as $x$. For $f:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}^m$ (vector output) it is a **Jacobian** ($m\times n$). Because a neural network always ends in a **scalar loss** $L\in\mathbb{R}$, we only ever compute gradients (same shape as the variable) — never full Jacobians.

questions.q: Why can backpropagation avoid computing full Jacobians, and what shape does the gradient of the loss w.r.t. a weight matrix have?
questions.answer:
Because the loss is a **scalar** $L\in\mathbb{R}$. The derivative of a scalar by a matrix is a **gradient with the same shape as that matrix** (each entry $\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_{ij}}$ is a scalar), not a Jacobian. So $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W}$ has the same shape as $W$.
A Jacobian would only appear for a **vector→vector** map (e.g. $\frac{\partial Y}{\partial W}$ alone), but the scalar loss collapses it.
questions.extend: Shape-matching rule: $\frac{\partial L}{\partial v}$ has the same shape as $v$, for any variable $v$. This is the single most useful sanity check in backprop.

questions.q: Distinguish a gradient from a Jacobian. Give the shapes for $f:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}$ and $f:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}^m$.
questions.answer: **Gradient** (scalar output $f:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}$): a vector $\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\in\mathbb{R}^n$ (same shape as input). **Jacobian** (vector output $f:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}^m$): a matrix $\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times n}$ (one row per output, one column per input).
questions.extend: Scalar-out → gradient (input-shaped). Vector-out → Jacobian (out×in). Deep learning lives in the gradient world thanks to the scalar loss.

questions.q: For a layer $y=f(x)$ with $x\in\mathbb{R}^n,\ y\in\mathbb{R}^m$, what is the shape of $\frac{\partial y}{\partial x}$? After composing with a scalar loss $L$, what is the shape of $\frac{\partial L}{\partial x}$?
questions.answer: $\frac{\partial y}{\partial x}$ is the **Jacobian**, shape $m\times n$. After the scalar loss, $\frac{\partial L}{\partial x}$ is a **gradient** of shape $n$ (same as $x$): $\frac{\partial L}{\partial x}=\big(\frac{\partial y}{\partial x}\big)^\top\frac{\partial L}{\partial y}$.
questions.extend: Vector→vector gives a Jacobian; the scalar loss contracts it into an input-shaped gradient.

knowledge_points.title: Affine-Layer Derivatives
knowledge_points.recap: For $Y=XW+b$ with upstream gradient $\text{dout}=\frac{\partial L}{\partial Y}$ (shape $N\times M$): $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W}=X^\top\,\text{dout}$ ($D\times M$), $\frac{\partial L}{\partial X}=\text{dout}\,W^\top$ ($N\times D$), $\frac{\partial L}{\partial b}=\mathbf{1}_N^\top\,\text{dout}=\sum_{n}\text{dout}$ ($1\times M$).

questions.q: For $Y=XW+b$ ($X_{N\times D}$, $W_{D\times M}$, $b_{1\times M}$) and a scalar loss $L$, derive $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W}$ and $\frac{\partial L}{\partial X}$ with their shapes.
questions.answer:
$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W}=X^\top\cdot\frac{\partial L}{\partial Y}=X^\top\,\text{dout}$, shape $D\times M$ (same as $W$).
$\frac{\partial L}{\partial X}=\frac{\partial L}{\partial Y}\cdot W^\top=\text{dout}\,W^\top$, shape $N\times D$ (same as $X$).
Both hold **because $L$ is a scalar** — otherwise $\frac{\partial Y}{\partial W}$ would be a Jacobian tensor.
questions.extend: Memorize by shape: to get $D\times M$ from $X(N\times D)$ and $\text{dout}(N\times M)$ you must use $X^\top\text{dout}$. The transpose and order are forced by shape-matching.

questions.q: How is the bias $b$ handled in $Y=XW+b$ (it's $1\times M$ but $XW$ is $N\times M$), and what is $\frac{\partial L}{\partial b}$?
questions.answer:
$b$ is **broadcast**: mathematically $Y=XW+\mathbf{1}_N b$, where $\mathbf{1}_N$ is a column of ones that copies $b$ across all $N$ rows (this is exactly NumPy broadcasting).
Treating $\mathbf{1}_N b$ like another $XW$, $\frac{\partial L}{\partial b}=\mathbf{1}_N^\top\cdot\frac{\partial L}{\partial Y}=\sum_{i=1}^{N}\text{dout}_{i,:}$ — i.e. **sum the upstream gradient over the batch** → shape $1\times M$.
questions.extend: Bias gradient = column-sum of dout (`np.sum(dout, axis=0)`), because $b$ contributed identically to every row of the batch.

questions.q: Why is the per-weight derivative $\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_{u,v}}=\sum_i\sum_j\frac{\partial L}{\partial y_{i,j}}\frac{\partial y_{i,j}}{\partial w_{u,v}}$ a double sum?
questions.answer: Because $w_{u,v}$ can influence **multiple** entries $y_{i,j}$ of the output, and the chain rule must accumulate the contribution through **every** output entry it affects. The scalar $\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_{u,v}}$ sums all those paths — this is the 'each neuron collects/sums the upstream gradients it took part in' picture.
questions.extend: Each parameter's gradient = sum over all outputs it touched. For $Y=XW$, $w_{u,v}$ only affects column $v$, which is why the matrix form collapses to $X^\top\text{dout}$.

questions.q: Given $X\in\mathbb{R}^{4\times3}$, $W\in\mathbb{R}^{3\times5}$ and $\text{dout}\in\mathbb{R}^{4\times5}$, state the shapes of $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W}$, $\frac{\partial L}{\partial X}$, $\frac{\partial L}{\partial b}$ and how to compute each.
questions.extend: Each gradient matches its variable's shape (W:3×5, X:4×3, b:1×5). Use that to decide transposes/multiplication order without re-deriving.

knowledge_points.title: Chain Rule & the Upstream Gradient (dout)
knowledge_points.recap: Backprop treats each layer in its **own scope**. The upstream gradient $\text{dout}=\frac{\partial L}{\partial Y}$ (already computed by the layers above) enters the layer; the layer computes its parameter gradients and passes a new upstream gradient $\frac{\partial L}{\partial X}$ to the layer below.

questions.q: Explain the 'upstream gradient' (dout) abstraction in backpropagation for a layer like Affine → ReLU → Affine → Sigmoid → Loss.
questions.answer: When deriving a given layer (say the Affine layer), we treat it as a function with its own **scope**. Everything above it has already produced $\text{dout}=\frac{\partial L}{\partial Y}$ (the **upstream gradient**). The layer uses dout to compute its local parameter gradients ($\frac{\partial L}{\partial W}, \frac{\partial L}{\partial b}$) and the gradient to send further down ($\frac{\partial L}{\partial X}$), which becomes the next layer's dout.
questions.extend: Modular backprop: each layer only needs its forward inputs + the incoming dout. This local abstraction is exactly how autograd frameworks implement `backward()`.

questions.q: Compute a tiny chain-rule example: $f(X)=2X$, $g(z)=\sum_i z_i$, $L=g(f(X))$ with $X\in\mathbb{R}^n$. What is $\frac{\partial L}{\partial x_1}$?
questions.answer: $L=2x_1+2x_2+\dots+2x_n$. Then $\frac{\partial L}{\partial x_1}=\sum_k\frac{\partial L}{\partial f(x)_k}\frac{\partial f(x)_k}{\partial x_1}=2\cdot1+2\cdot0+\dots+2\cdot0=\mathbf{2}$ (only $f(x)_1=2x_1$ depends on $x_1$).
questions.extend: The chain rule sums over every intermediate that depends on $x_1$; here only one term survives because $f$ is element-wise.

questions.q: In a Conv→ReLU→Conv block, the second conv receives dout. List what it must compute and what it passes to the ReLU below it.
questions.answer: The second conv uses its incoming $\text{dout}=\frac{\partial L}{\partial Y}$ to compute its **parameter gradients** ($\frac{\partial L}{\partial W},\frac{\partial L}{\partial b}$) and its **input gradient** $\frac{\partial L}{\partial X}$; it then passes $\frac{\partial L}{\partial X}$ **down** as the dout for the ReLU below.
questions.extend: Each layer: receive dout → compute local param grads → emit input-grad as the next layer's dout. This locality is exactly how autograd works.
